item.title: Issues with First Quantization Quantum Mechanics on Curved Space-times
item.description:
If you want to read the thesis document itself, you can find it here.

During my senior year at Reed I worked with Prof. Joel Franklin on a thesis project. It started as a humble attempt to describe the gravitational interaction of photons that behaved according to the quantum mechanical wave-function for single photons (see here) and ended with an investigation of the reasons for the absence of bound states when treating the Dirac equation around black holes with an event horizon. Along the way I studied a variety of tools and topics in math and physics including, among others, the spectral theory of differential operators, the RAGE and ergodic theorems (thanks to Prof. Tom Wieting), self-adjoint extensions, spinors, the BTZ black hole, the tetrad formalism, gravitational waves, Hawking radiation, and Chandrasekhar separation.

The thesis at Reed is supposed to be accessible to one’s peers; that is, fellow senior undergraduates. Indeed, most incoming physics seniors (including myself) peruse theses from years past for ideas and inspiration in the search for a thesis topic. Joel helped me realize that a very compelling story could be told with some of the work I had done throughout the year: a story about a search for bound states. For the sake of the narrative there is much that I did throughout the year that is not contained in the final document. The tone throughout, but especially in the introduction and conclusion, is very casual and is meant to set the stage for the story. This thesis is not structured so much as a presentation of research findings as it is a narrative of intellectual discovery and struggle. My hope is that by providing the undergraduate reader with an understanding of the reasons why I chose to take a certain path through the space of possible research programs they will be more engaged and will come away with an understanding of not only why the Dirac equation does not admit bound states in space-times with a horizon, but also what it means to do research in theoretical physics. It is also my hope that readers with a more advanced background will find content of interest in this thesis, as several intriguing questions and points are raised, including: equivalent notions of bound states, co-ordinate invariant spectral properties of differential operators, and why closed geodesics are lost when the Dirac equation is introduced.

This attempt at presenting material that most would consider abstruse in an engaging, and hopefully different, manner has also awakened in me a desire to become a better writer and communicator of science.

item.title: What is Tohuvavohu?
item.description:
The first one-and-a-half lines of Genesis go as follows:

…בראשית ברא אלהים את השמים ואת הארץ. והארץ היתה תהו ובהו וחשך על־פני תהום

In the Hebrew text above, the phrase ‘Tohu va-vohu’ is in bold, the 3rd and 4th words of the second sentence (right-to-left). These lines are commonly translated as:

“In the beginning, God created the Heavens and the Earth. And the Earth was Tohu va-vohu and darkness was on the face of the deep…”

Despite opening this post with a quote from scripture, I am not a religious person. I am intensely skeptical of religion and am probably best characterized as fiercely agnostic. However, I was raised in a Jewish household and spent the first 12 years of my life studying religious texts. It seems like it would be a waste not to utilize some of that knowledge at least occasionally. Only two things have really stayed with me from those early years:

  1. How to calculate the proper compensation if your donkey falls into a hole I dug and failed to properly cover.
  2. The phrase Tohu va-vohu.

So, why do I find these words so interesting? And why have I failed to translate them yet?

The answers to these two questions are interrelated. Even without knowing what Tohu va-vohu means, we see from context that it describes the initial conditions of the Universe. As someone who studies physics, and is especially interested in cosmology, I find this compelling in itself. I am not claiming that there is any hidden mystical truth about the initial state of the Universe in these words, only that the concept of an intial state as opposed to creatio ex nihilo is intriguing in the religious context where we are so often told to accept a divine creator, as opposed to an orderer. Indeed, this debate goes back at least as far as Rabbi Akiva who has a classic argument in the B’reshit Rabbah refuting the gnostic view that God needed the use of, or assistance from, some primordial materials (the Tohu and the Bohu) in order to build the Universe. So, it is clear that the meaning of these words is critical to these arguments. If Tohu and Bohu are qualities that imply some form or substance, this would give ample weight to the Gnostic viewpoint.

So, on to the task of translation. This turns out to be rather difficult. The phrase Tohu va-vohu appears only three times total in the entire canon: the instance we have been discussing (Genesis 1:2), Jeremiah 4:23, and Isaiah 34:11. So, we have a very small sample set to work with in attempting translation. Moreover, the issue is compounded because the occasions where Tohu va-vohu appear in Jeremiah and Isaiah actually seem to be directly referencing the use of the words in Genesis 1:2. In Jeremiah 4:23:

.ראיתי את־הארץ והנה־תהו ובהו ואל־השמים ואין אורם

“And I looked at the Earth and saw it was Tohu va-vohu, and at the Heavens and they were without light.”

In context, this is part of a longer passage where Jeremiah is describing an undoing of creation. An explicit reversal of the acts carried out in the first few lines of Genesis. So this can not be treated as a separate source from which to derive some meaning for the phrase. In Isaiah 34:11:

.וירשוה קאת וקפוד וינשוף וערב ישכנו־בה ונטה עליה קו־תהו ואבני־בהו

“But the cormorant and the bittern will possess it, and the owl and the raven will dwell in it, and he shall stretch out on it the line of Tohu and the stones of Bohu.”

This passage is also part of a description of desolation and destruction. This time a prediction of what is to befall Babylon, as if Babylon will be unmade. Even here, with a connection to other words which we can translate easily (‘line’ and ‘stones’), it is very unclear what information we can glean from this passage. If anything, the connection of Tohu and Bohu to material things gives some weight to the Gnostic argument.

The reader familiar with Genesis will know that the overwhelmingly predominant translation of Tohu va-vohu is “without form and void,” or something of similar meaning. This translation, which treats Tohu va-vohu as a hendiadys, supports the canonical view of creatio ex nihilo. Even the Septuagint, our earliest translation out of the biblical Hebrew, translates Tohu va-vohu as “ἀόρατος καὶ ἀκατα-σκεύαστος” which means “invisible and unformed”. It is important to note that this translation was prepared by 70 scholars who, while much more learned than myself, were also very much part of the majority camp which believed in a creatio ex nihilo.

Regardless, as we have seen there is really not much support for any translation at all. The leading interpretation seems to be based on oral tradition, what Jews would call Torah she b’al peh (תורה שבעל פה). My personal understanding of Tohu va-vohu is as something akin to chaos (which is not an entirely improper translation from the Greek in the Septuagint). I like to think of the creation myth as Order emerging from the Chaos, as this ties together nicely with my interest in emergence and symmetry breaking. The fact that Tohu va-vohu is untranslateable and thus the initial state of the Universe is, in a sense, unknowable from religious sources informed my early interest in science. I was very skeptical even as a young child, and I remember studying these words at the age of 6, seeing that there was debate over their interpretation, and falling down the rabbit hole of ancient rabbinical debates until I realized that the ony way out was through science.

So, when I use the words Tohu va-vohu I am referring to the initial state of the universe, before the ‘Creation’, and I am affirming the unique ability of science to bring us knowledge. This phrase has meant a great deal to me over the years, and at one point I even filled out name change paperwork to make it my surname.

As a final note: you may find the Wikipedia page for this phrase at Tohu wa-Bohu. It is my firm belief that this transliteration of wa-bohu vs. va-vohu is incorrect and does not reflect the proper pronunciation of the words. To hear me saying these words, you can click here.

item.title: Simulating Quantum Algorithms
item.description:
TL;DR: Check this out.

This project began at the 2016 Wolfram Summer Science School, where I was a participant. We had been told to arrive with a project idea in mind, and having just finished working my way through Maximilian Schlosshauer’s fantastic book Decoherence and the Quantum-to-Classical Transition, I naturally had the idea of simulating the decoherence of some quantum system, and perhaps demonstrating how the decohering timescale changes for systems of different size. My thoughts on this matter were also stimulated by a very interesting, but ultimately dissapointing, talk that Dr. Michael Kellman gave at Reed in the Fall of 2015 where he presented the results of this paper. While he didn’t talk about decoherence, his talk inspired in me an interest in simulating quantum systems, and my fascination with Max’s book demanded a need to know more about decoherence (which hadn’t been mentioned at all in my undergraduate quantum courses).

However, the moment I entered the room for my project meeting with Dr. Stephen Wolfram, he already had a different idea in mind. The idea was to build functionality into Mathematica for easily building and simulating quantum algorithms that was powerful enough for researchers to use and simple enough to be used as an educational tool. At the time I wasn’t aware that only a month earlier IBM had launched their Quantum Experience platform, but I’m sure Stephen was. I immediately realized that this was a great idea that would allow me to explore my interest in simulating quantum systems, while also forcing me to think hard about design and usability (something scientists aren’t known for), and that would necessitate a solid grasp of the subject matter. I began immediately, and spent the next 3 weeks working in a frenzy. You can read about the state of the project by the end of the summer school here. Suffice to say, it worked.

After the summer school I was asked to continue work on this project to extend the functionality and prepare it for ultimate inclusion in the Wolfram Language technology stack. This work is ongoing.

item.title: about
item.description:
Biography

I am currently a member of the Science Operations Team for the NASA Swift Explorer mission based at Penn State. I also work with Wolfram building tools for quantum computing simulations. Before coming to Penn State I was an undergraduate at Reed College where I studied physics and wrote a thesis on quantum mechanics in curved space-times under the supervision of Dr. Joel Franklin. In the summer of 2015 I was supervised by Dr. Kevork Abazajian at the University of California, Irvine where we studied ways to measure the combined neutrino mass using cosmological probes of Large Scale Structure. I have also done research on the limits and applicability of digital simulations of highly chaotic systems. I aspire to be a better writer, and – along with descriptions of past and current projects – you may occasionally find articles I am writing on this site.

In previous lives I worked as a custom frame builder, composite repair technician, mechanic, apprentice shipwright, and climbing guide.
